growth_records.strain: 1A01 / 3D05 (coastal ocean isolates, Canoe Beach, Nahant MA)
growth_records.agit: 145 rpm (preculture Marine Broth); stirred IsoCaRB bioreactor for experiments

up.units: simple sugar taken up directly (no extracellular hydrolysis); consumed over incubation, not quantified as specific rate

sec.units: ug C L-1 min-1 (maximum volumetric respiration rate, peaking within first 12-24 h)
sec.method: online infrared CO2 analyzer (Sable Systems CA-10) + manometric trap yields

growth_records.cite: McNichol et al. Dynamics of carbon substrate competition among heterotrophic microorganisms. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae018. [PMC10942773]
growth_records.snippet: The highest rates of CO2 production were observed during coculture experiments with glucosamine that had a maximum CO2 production rate of 69 ug C L-1 min-1 (Fig. 1A).
growth_records.notes: Co-culture (cannot attribute per species). Substrate: 2.2 g glucosamine-HCl in 2 L T-R minimal medium = 1.1 g/L (~730 mg C total = 365 mg C/L, stated). Max CO2 production (secretion) = 69 ug C/L/min (volumetric, not per gDW; peaks 12-24 h then declines to near-constant within 4-5 days). Aerobic ~20% O2, room temperature (~22 C). No explicit growth rate reported (cell density tracked by dPCR but no numeric mu). T-R salt recipe only in Table S3 -> medium_composition_stated:false.

growth_records.agit: 145 rpm (preculture Marine Broth); stirred IsoCaRB bioreactor for experiments
growth_records.medium: T-R minimal medium + Guaymas Basin hydrothermally-influenced sediment (mixed labile+complex organic matter)

up.compound: sedimentary organic matter (complex + labile: petroleum hydrocarbons, organic acids, phytoplankton-derived C)
up.units: remineralized; not quantified as specific rate

sec.units: ug C L-1 min-1 (maximum volumetric respiration rate)
sec.method: online IR CO2 analyzer + manometric trap yields

growth_records.cite: McNichol et al. Dynamics of carbon substrate competition among heterotrophic microorganisms. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae018. [PMC10942773]
growth_records.snippet: the CO2 production rate ... significantly higher during incubation with hydrothermal sediment compared to unimpacted sediment, reaching a maximum rate of 2.9 ug C L-1 min-1 and 1.4 ug C L-1 min-1, respectively.
growth_records.notes: Co-culture on complex natural substrate. ~22 g dry decarbonated sediment, TOC ~3.5% -> ~770 mg C (~385 mg C/L stated). Max CO2 secretion = 2.9 ug C/L/min (volumetric). Aerobic ~20% O2, room temp (~22 C). No numeric growth rate. Undefined organic substrate -> medium_composition_stated:false.

growth_records.agit: 145 rpm (preculture Marine Broth); stirred IsoCaRB bioreactor for experiments
growth_records.medium: T-R minimal medium + Guaymas Basin unimpacted (cool) sediment (predominantly complex organic matter)

up.compound: sedimentary organic matter (complex: aged terrigenous / phytoplankton-derived C)

sec.units: ug C L-1 min-1 (maximum volumetric respiration rate)
sec.method: online IR CO2 analyzer + manometric trap yields

growth_records.cite: McNichol et al. Dynamics of carbon substrate competition among heterotrophic microorganisms. The ISME Journal (2024). DOI:10.1093/ismejo/wrae018. [PMC10942773]
growth_records.snippet: reaching a maximum rate of 2.9 ug C L-1 min-1 and 1.4 ug C L-1 min-1, respectively (Fig. 1) ... Cocultures resulted in higher respiration rates and remineralization of organic matter.
growth_records.notes: Co-culture on complex (unimpacted cool sediment) substrate; max CO2 secretion = 1.4 ug C/L/min (volumetric), lowest of the three substrates. ~22 g dry sediment, TOC ~3.5% -> ~385 mg C/L. Aerobic ~20% O2, room temp. No numeric growth rate. Undefined substrate -> medium_composition_stated:false.

growth_records.medium: MBL marine minimal medium (bicarbonate-buffered) with colloidal chitin as sole carbon source

up.units: g/L consumed to completion (batch total; residual < 62 mg/L detection limit)
up.method: substrate depletion / elemental carbon balance in bioreactor

sec.units: % of input chitin carbon (unaccounted balance attributed to respired CO2)
sec.method: carbon balance by elemental analysis (100% - biomass - EPS - LMW)

sec.compound: EPS (extracellular polymeric substances, >3 kDa; proteins + polysaccharides + humic-like)
sec.units: % of input chitin carbon (=~45 mg dried EPS per g chitin consumed)
sec.method: elemental analysis of dialyzed freeze-dried EPS fraction
elemental analysis of 3 kDa-filtrate fraction (bicarbonate buffer allowed DIC removal before C quantification)

yields.Yxs_biomass_carbon: 33-36% of input chitin carbon recovered in biomass

product_yields.EPS_carbon: 4.1-4.5% of chitin carbon (~45 mg EPS / g chitin consumed)
product_yields.EPS_fraction_of_exuded_C: ~25% of total exuded carbon (EPS + LMW), i.e. EPS ~one-quarter of secreted carbon
product_yields.EPS_composition: proteins 13-23% (g/g) of EPS; hydrolysable polysaccharides 9-36% of EPS carbon (glucose ~55% of monomers); DNA ~0.17% (g/g)

growth_records.cite: Pontrelli, Sammy et al. Degradation of extracellular polymeric substances shapes microbial community diversity. PLOS Biology (2025). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.3003287. [PMC12306770]
growth_records.method: not reported (growth followed by OD600 to early stationary phase; no specific growth rate or generation time given)
growth_records.snippet: Of the 2 g/L chitin that were completely consumed (with residual concentrations below the detection limit of 62 mg/L), 33%-36% of the input chitin carbon was recovered in biomass, 4.1%-4.5% in EPS, and 11%-14% in LMW metabolites, while the
growth_records.notes: Batch bicarbonate-buffered bioreactor, Vibrio sp. 1A01 on 2 g/L colloidal chitin as sole C source, 27C, pH 7.8, early stationary phase. Values are a CLOSED CARBON BUDGET from elemental analysis of two replicate bioreactors, NOT time-resolved rates: biomass 33-36%, EPS 4.1-4.5%, LMW 11-14%, CO2 45-52% (by difference, sum=100%) of the input chitin carbon. EPS yield also expressed gravimetrically as ~45 mg dried EPS per g chitin consumed (Fig 1B); two other chitin degraders gave similar 4-7% EPS yields (figure-only, not tabulated). No specific growth rate (mu), doubling time, dilution rate, or per-gDW rates are reported, so mu cannot be derived (only endpoint OD600 to stationary was tracked; time not given). No qS in mmol/gDW/h derivable without biomass concentration and duration. EPS composition: proteins 13-23% g/g, polysaccharides 9-36% of C (glucose ~55% of monomers), DNA ~0.17% g/g. Medium = MBL (K2HPO4 1 mM, Na2SO4 1 mM; 4x seawater salts giving final NaCl 20, MgCl2*6H2O 3, CaCl2*2H2O 0.15, KCl 0.5 g/L; trace minerals + vitamins) with 50 mM NaHCO3 replacing the 50 mM TES buffer used in the standard MBL recipe. Nitrogen for the bioreactor run is supplied by the chitin (GlcNAc) itself; separate yield/EPS-consumption assays added 1 mM NH4Cl. This is a genome-scale-model-relevant carbon-allocation dataset (biomass/EPS/LMW/CO2 split) for the marine chitin degrader Vibrio sp. 1A01, a model organism for marine carbon cycling.
